interop.title: MW-PWG-PWK Interoperability Hard Cases
interop.description: This tool shows 250 automatically sampled hard cases for the MW-PWG-PWK interoperability track.

demo.title: Worked cases, end to end
demo.description: Three contrasting CDSL entries, traced live through the workbench: raw records → dictionary-neutral model → the loss reports that record what each standard cannot hold → the csl: construct that answers them. Pick a case; every field below is read from the regenerable artifacts.
demo.stage0-note: The same headword, digitised independently in each dictionary, in CDSL's project-specific pseudo-markup (truncated).
demo.stage1-note: One commensurable record that both profiles and all loss reports derive from, so they stay comparable. The phenomena tags are what the sampler selected the case for.
demo.stage4: Stages 2–4 — what each model loses, and the csl: remedy
demo.stage4-note: Every degradation recorded as an evidence-bound report. “answered by” names the implemented, schema-validated csl: construct (pySHACL for OntoLex, jing for TEI); a dash means the string is preserved but its semantics are not.
demo.asymmetry: Read together they show the asymmetry in miniature: TEI stays partial (it keeps everything as text but cannot type the hedge or the root role), OntoLex is never clean (it must model or drop), and the neutral lane records an editorial loss that is neither standard's fault.
demo.reproduce: Every field comes straight from npm run build-pilot; the full per-case TEI/OntoLex files and the narrative are on GitHub.
demo.blurb-ac: A verbal root that exercises every loss family at once: a root not a word, the MW L. lexicographer hedge, a PWG→PWK abridged citation apparatus, and named sources that include both an indigenous kośa authority and plain textual coordinates. It attaches all the optional dictionaries.
demo.blurb-annavid: The compound anna (“food”) + vid, where the three dictionaries disagree on the second member — MW reads “knowing”, PWG “possessing”, PWK “acquiring”. Derivation maps cleanly into TEI; the losses cluster instead in the OntoLex graph and the PWG→PWK→MW editorial collapse.
demo.blurb-Ayana: A suppressed headword: MW prints only a pointer, “(for 1. Āyana See under Aya.)”, while PWG carries the full entry with three Vedic attestations and PWK a different homonym. Modeled honestly as a conjectured continuation rather than an asserted parent.

lossAnalysis.title: Loss Analysis
lossAnalysis.description: Quantitative analysis of the loss-report corpus and the MW/PWG/PWK lineage, regenerated by npm run analyze-loss.
lossAnalysis.target-status: Target × status: how the models fail
lossAnalysis.asymmetry-note: For the Western MW/PWG/PWK cases TEI is never lossy and OntoLex is never clean — the models succeed differently. The neutral lane (the dictionary lineage itself) is the most lossy. The only TEI-lossy reports are the indigenous kośa sense/citation fusions.
